elements.sent-2411-001: Recent advances in code-specific large language models (LLMs) have greatly enhanced code generation and refinement capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the safety of code LLMs remains under-explored, posing potential risks as insecure code generated by these models may introduce vulnerabilities into real-world systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: Previous work proposes to collect security-focused instruction-tuning dataset from real-world vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-004: It is constrained by the data sparsity of vulnerable code, and has limited applicability in the iterative post-training workflows of modern LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we propose \toolname, a novel proactive security alignment approach designed to align code LLMs with secure coding practices.
elements.sent-2411-006: The scenarios synthesized by \toolname triggers 25 times more vulnerable code than a normal instruction-tuning dataset, resulting in a security-focused alignment dataset 7 times larger than the previous work.
elements.sent-2411-007: Experiments show that models trained with \toolname is 29.2\% to 35.5\% more secure compared to previous work, with a marginal negative effect of less than 2\% on model's utility.
elements.sent-2411-008: Large language models (LLMs) capable of generating code based on human instructions have revolutionized programming by significantly facilitating tasks such as code generation~\cite{zhu2024deepseek and refinement~\cite{zheng2024opencodeinterpreter,guo2024exploring .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: By leveraging unsupervised pretraining on vast corpora and subsequent fine-tuning (post-training, e.g., supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with human feedback, and preference tuning), code-specific LLMs have progressively improved their ability to generate better code.~\looseness=-1 Compared to the intense attention given to enhancing safety, truthfulness, and ethical considerations in general LLMs during post-training~\cite{ganguli2022red,liu2023training,ji2024beavertails,dubey2024llama,hurst2024gpt , the security implications of code-specific LLMs remain under-addressed.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Insecure code generation by these models has been shown to introduce vulnerabilities, posing risks in real-world applications~\cite{pearce2021empirical,pearce2022asleep .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Recent studies reveal that even state-of-the-art code LLMs frequently generate insecure code~\cite{he2023large,bhatt2023purple,heinstruction , highlighting the urgent need for targeted security alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Early efforts, such as SafeCoder~\cite{heinstruction , seek to address security concerns during the instruction-tuning phase by constructing datasets of vulnerable code and corresponding fixes from GitHub commits.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The security-focused dataset is then integrated with standard instruction-tuning datasets to teach the model to generate secure code.
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite SafeCoder's improvement of secure code generation with limited sacrifice of utility, instruction-tuning on the security code dataset collected from real-world programs faces two critical challenges:.
elements.sent-2411-015: Vulnerable code snippets in real-world programs and their fixes are often sparse and highly contextual.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, SafeCoder collects 465 entries from 145 million git commits.
elements.sent-2411-017: The sparsity limits the effectiveness and generalizability of training secure code LLMs from real-world vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-018: Coupling security alignment with the standard instruction-tuning phase restricts its utility in modern LLM training workflows.
elements.sent-2411-019: Code LLMs can undergo iterative post-training processes based on human feedback for further performance improvements.
elements.sent-2411-020: Reverting to the instruction-tuning stage for security alignment necessitates retraining, which is resource-intensive and risks discarding the benefits of prior post-training efforts.
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, recent study~\cite{tang2024understanding shows that the alignment training is more effective if the alignment data are within the distribution of the target code LLM, so it may not be ideal to simply reuse the security-focused instruction-tuning dataset in the alignment process.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we propose \toolname, a {\em proactive security alignment approach to improving the safety of a code LLM that has been post-trained with substantial efforts.}
elements.sent-2411-023: It fortifies code LLMs systematically by intentionally triggering and resolving vulnerabilities during post-training.
elements.sent-2411-024: It instructs the code LLM to generate both the vulnerable code and the corresponding fix under different generation contexts, and aligns the code LLM to secure coding practices with advanced preference learning objectives, minimizing negative effects to its utility.
elements.sent-2411-025: To address the challenge imposed by the sparsity of vulnerabilities in real-world code repositories, \toolname synthesizes instructions that may induce vulnerable code from a code LLM.
elements.sent-2411-026: The key observation of \toolname is that the Common Weakness Enumerations (CWEs)~\cite{cwe , which abstract diverse program vulnerabilities, offer a generalizable foundation for simulating how vulnerabilities manifest across various coding tasks and programming languages.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Specifically, \toolname synthesizes instructions that may expose the weakness of a code LLM by incorporating CWEs to a standard code instruction-tuning dataset with ChatGPT.
elements.sent-2411-028: To address the second challenge, \toolname employs a preference-learning paradigm, leveraging pairwise data generated from the code LLM.
elements.sent-2411-029: Given a code LLM, \toolname uses it to implement the synthesized coding instructions, and leverages commonly used vulnerability detectors to identify code snippets that violates secure coding practices.
elements.sent-2411-030: Then the same code LLM is prompted to fix the insecure code provided the feedback from the detectors.
elements.sent-2411-031: The vulnerable code and its fix are further used to align the code LLM with advanced preference learning objectives that align an LLM without degrading the model's overall capability.
elements.sent-2411-032: Empirically, the instructions synthesized by \toolname induces 25 times more vulnerable code than a standard instruction-tuning dataset.
elements.sent-2411-033: The alignment dataset generated by the proactive approach is 7 times larger than the SafeCoder dataset.
elements.sent-2411-034: We demonstrate the effectiveness of \toolname on the PurpleLlama~\cite{bhatt2023purple secure coding benchmark.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The models trained with the dataset synthesized by \toolname are 29.2\%--35.5\% more secure than ones trained with the SafeCoder dataset.
elements.sent-2411-036: We further validate that the effects of \toolname on the utility of code LLMs are less than 2 percentage points.
elements.sent-2411-037: We conduct thorough ablation studies to justify the design decisions in \toolname.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our work makes the following key contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-039: We introduce a novel post-training security alignment process for code LLMs, which systematically addresses security risks during code generation.
elements.sent-2411-040: We develop an automatic pipeline to synthesize proactive security alignment data given a code LLM and vulnerability types in a programming language.
elements.sent-2411-041: We publish a dataset of synthesized vulnerability-inducing instructions that can effectively expose the weakness of code LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-042: Through targeted security alignment, we demonstrate that \toolname improves the ability of code LLMs to generate secure code without compromising their general code generation capabilities, across multiple models, languages, and vulnerability types.
elements.sent-2411-043: Post-training refers to fine-tuning pre-trained LLMs on specialized datasets and objectives to enhance their capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-044: This process typically involves two key phases: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and preference tuning, such as Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF).
elements.sent-2411-045: During the SFT phase, models are trained on (instruction, response) pairs, enabling them to follow human instructions effectively~\cite{wang2022super,chung2024scaling,zhou2024lima,wang2023self .}
elements.sent-2411-046: In the preference-tuning phase, the model's behavior is further aligned with human preferences.
elements.sent-2411-047: The original RLHF framework, introduced by OpenAI~\cite{ouyang2022training , uses a reward model to guide this alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Alternative approaches, such as reward-free preference tuning~\cite{yuan2023rrhf,rafailov2024direct,shao2024deepseekmath,azar2024general , have also been explored in recent research.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Notably, the post-training pipelines for modern LLMs have grown increasingly intricate, involving larger-scale data, more sophisticated processes, and greater human effort~\cite{dubey2024llama,adler2024nemotron .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Therefore, it becomes difficult to inject specific instruction tuning stages to such LLMs post-training pipeline as SafeCoder does.
elements.sent-2411-051: While general-purpose LLMs are capable of generating code~\cite{hurst2024gpt,adler2024nemotron,dubey2024llama , considerable efforts are still directed towards the development of specialized coding models that are smaller in size but maintain competitive performance~\cite{lozhkov2024starcoder,zhu2024deepseek,huang2024opencoder .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Code language models have progressed significantly beyond basic function-level code completion~\cite{chen2021evaluating,rozière2024code , advancing to more sophisticated instruction-following capabilities that leverage contextual information across entire code repositories.}
elements.sent-2411-053: These advancements have been facilitated, in part, by instruction tuning specifically tailored for coding tasks~\cite{luo2023wizardcoder, azar2024general,wei2023magicoder .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Recently, alignment techniques have received increased attention, focusing on signals such as compiler feedback and execution outcomes to further improve model performance~\cite{gehring2024rlef,hui2024qwen2,wei2024selfcodealign .}
elements.sent-2411-055: As software development increasingly relies on LLM-generated code, there has been a growing emphasis on understanding and improving its security.
elements.sent-2411-056: Early empirical studies have demonstrated that commercial products such as GitHub Copilot can result in obscurity and even vulnerability issues in code~\cite{pearce2021empirical,pearce2022asleep .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Several benchmarks have been developed recently, including SecurityEval~\cite{siddiq2022securityeval , LLMSecEval~\cite{tony2023llmseceval , and the Purple Llama CyberSecEval benchmark~\cite{bhatt2023purple , which provide standardized approaches for evaluating the security of LLM-generated code.}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: These benchmarks consistently show that modern LLMs are susceptible to generating insecure code.
elements.sent-2411-059: To mitigate the risks associated with LLM-generated vulnerabilities, recent work has focused on refining the training process and incorporating safety measures.
elements.sent-2411-060: SVEN~\cite{he2023large and SafeCoder~\cite{heinstruction propose methods to improve the security of code generation by fine-tuning LLMs with real-world vulnerable and secure code training data.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: APILOT~\cite{bai2024apilot addresses the issue of outdated or insecure API use by implementing a mechanism to sidestep deprecated APIs, thereby reducing potential security threats.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Additionally, INDICT~\cite{le2024indict introduces an actor-critic agent system with internal critique dialogues to enhance the security and helpfulness of generated code through iterative feedback.}
elements.sent-2411-063: CodeFavor~\cite{liu2024learning proposes a code preference model that can predict whether a snippet of code is in conform of secure coding practices.}
elements.sent-2411-064: However, it is not designed for code generation.
elements.sent-2411-065: Different from previous work, \toolname focus on strengthening the ability of Code LLMs that has been fully pos-trained to directly generate safe code, without going through complex agentic workflows during inference, and is not limited to specific vulnerability types or APIs.
elements.sent-2411-066: Suppose that an organization decides to deploy a code LLM $\pi_{\theta (y|x)=\Pi_{i}\pi_{\theta (y_i|y_{<i ,x)$ that is pre-trained and post-trained with non-trivial efforts into production.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: However, the organization needs to incorporate certain security-related coding practices (e.g., sanitizing inputs to prevent command injections) before the code LLM can be safely used.
elements.sent-2411-068: For each programming language, there are a set of commonly occurred problems that may make the code vulnerable, namely CWEs~\cite{cwe , each associated with a set of good (safe) and bad (problematic) coding practices.}
elements.sent-2411-069: We denote all the combinations of programming language $l$ and CWE $c$ of size $N$ that an organization pays attention to as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{cwe}} = \{(l^{(i)}, c^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^N\end{equation} \newcommand{\dcwe {\ensuremath{\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{cwe }\xspace Following previous work~\cite{bhatt2023purple , we assume that the organization has a static analyzer that can detect whether a snippet of code follows secure code patterns.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Specifically, the static analyzer takes as input a code snippet, and outputs a list of detected CWEs.
elements.sent-2411-071: If the static analyzer returns an empty list, it implies the given code conforms with the secure coding practices of this organization.
elements.sent-2411-072: Formally, we denote the static analyzer as: \begin{equation}S: \mathcal{Y} \rightarrow \emptyset \cup \mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{cwe}} \cup \mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{cwe}}\times\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{cwe}}\cup\cdots\end{equation} To align $\pi_{\theta}$ with the secure coding practices, the organization needs a pairwise dataset for training.
elements.sent-2411-073: Each data entry consists of a coding instruction $x$, a preferred coding sample $y_w$, and a less-preferred coding sample $y_l$.
elements.sent-2411-074: The alignment training guides the code LLM to generate win samples with higher probabilities than to generate lose samples.
elements.sent-2411-075: We formally denote the alignment dataset as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{A} = \{(x^{(i)}, y_{w}^{(i)}, y_{l}^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^M,\end{equation}.
elements.section-2411-004: \toolname: Proactive Security Alignment of Code LLMs
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{figs/wf.pdf} \caption{How \toolname synthesizes the secure code alignment dataset. The data synthesis pipeline takes as input a {\em normal coding instruction} and a language/CWE pair, and produces an {\em error-inducing instruction} that may trigger the corresponding CWE. \toolname then uses the code LLM to implement the normal and error-inducing instructions respectively, yielding a snippet of {\em normal code} and a snippet of {\em vulnerable code}. \toolname further instructs the code LLM to fix the vulnerability in the vulnerable code, resulting the corresponding {\em fixed code}. The dataset used by \toolname consists both (1)~the vulnerable/fix code pairs to align the code LLM on secure coding practices, and (2)~the fix/normal code pairs to prevent the code LLM from overfitting.} \label{fig:wf}
elements.sent-2411-076: An overview of \toolname's data synthesis pipeline is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:wf .}
elements.sent-2411-077: It synthesizes error-inducing coding instructions from the normal programming tasks in an instruction-tuning dataset.
elements.sent-2411-078: Then it generate normal, vulnerable, and fixed code snippets with the code LLM.
elements.sent-2411-079: The alignment dataset contains both pairs of fixed~(win) and vulnerable~(lose) code, and pairs of normal~(win) and fixed~(lose) code.
elements.sent-2411-080: The former aligns the code LLM with secure coding practices, while the later prevents the code LLM from overfitting to secure coding patterns, mitigating the effects to the utility of the code LLM.
elements.sent-2411-081: We discuss how \toolname synthesizes error-inducing instructions in Section~\ref{sec:method:err-inducing , and discuss how it constructs alignment datasets and formulates the training objectives in Section~\ref{sec:method:align-data .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Previous efforts~\cite{wei2023magicoder,InfinityInstruct2024 have focused on synthesizing high-quality coding instructions for instruction tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-083: However, these synthesized instructions may not effectively capture potential weaknesses in generating secure code.
elements.sent-2411-084: This limitation arises because many CWEs are triggered by highly specific coding scenarios that are often not represented in standard instruction-tuning datasets.
elements.sent-2411-085: For example, CWE-79 shown in Figure~\ref{fig:wf denotes {\em Cross-Site Scripting .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: It describes the scenario that users inputs are directly embedded into web pages without proper sanitation.
elements.sent-2411-087: A malicious attacker can then execute arbitrary code in a victim's browser.
elements.sent-2411-088: To expose CWE-79 from the code LLM, the coding task has to be about writing a web application.
elements.sent-2411-089: Empirically, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:tigger-dist , only 0.7\% of a normal instruction-tuning dataset can trigger CWEs.}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Error-inducing instruction generation} \label{alg:err-inducing} \begin{algorithmic}[1] $: a standard instruction tuning dataset} \OUTPUT{$\mathcal{E}$: a set of error-inducing instructions. Each entry contains $l,c,i_{n},i_{e}$, denoting the programming language, the CWE, the normal instruction, and the error-inducing instruction, respectively.} \STATE $\mathcal{E} \leftarrow \emptyset$ \FOR{$l,c \in \dcwe $} \STATE $ scenario \leftarrow $ {\tt query\_cwe\_definition}$(l,c)$ \label{alg:err-inducing:scen} \STATE $\mathcal{I}_r \leftarrow $ {\tt relevant\_instruction}$(\mathcal{I}, l, c)$ \label{alg:err-inducing:rel} \STATE $\mathcal{E}_0 \leftarrow \emptyset$ \FOR{$i_n \in \mathcal{I}_r$} \STATE $i_e \leftarrow $ {\tt compose}$(i_n, scenario, l, c)$ \label{alg:err-inducing:compose} \STATE $\mathcal{E}_0 \leftarrow \mathcal{E}_0 \cup \{(l, c, i_n, i_e)\}$ \ENDFOR \STATE $\mathcal{E} \leftarrow \mathcal{E}\ \cup $ cluster$(\mathcal{E}_0, K)$ \label{alg:err-inducing:cluster} \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-090: Therefore, \toolname proposes to synthesize error-inducing coding instructions by fusing CWE-related program scenarios into normal instructions.
elements.sent-2411-091: We describe how \toolname synthesize error-inducing instructions in Algorithm~\ref{alg:err-inducing .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Given a programming language and a CWE, \toolname queries ChatGPT to enumerate program scenarios that might trigger the CWE in the corresponding language~(line~4).
elements.sent-2411-093: On the other hand, \toolname select the instructions that are relevant to the programming language from the instruction-tuning dataset~(line~4).
elements.sent-2411-094: For each relevant normal instruction, \toolname then instructs ChatGPT to compose the error-inducing instructions by combining the normal instruction with the program scenarios that may trigger the vulnerability~(line~7).
elements.sent-2411-095: The red block in the left part of Figure~\ref{fig:wf shows a concrete example.}
elements.sent-2411-096: The prompts used are in Appendix~\ref{appx:prompts .}
elements.sent-2411-097: One practical challenge is that the LLM may generate duplicate coding scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-098: For example, for the CWE-79 shown in Figure~\ref{fig:wf , the LLM may come up with the scenario ``a web application that displays strings from a user'' for multiple times.}
elements.sent-2411-099: It harms the diversity of the resulting dataset and increases the resource consumption in the alignment training.
elements.sent-2411-100: To mitigate the issue, we sample multiple answers for each query with a high temperature, and clustering all instructions relevant to a language/CWE to $K$ clusters.
elements.sent-2411-101: Then \toolname only leverages the centroid of each cluster, as denoted by line~10 in Algorithm~\ref{alg:err-inducing .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Figure~\ref{fig:inst-cluster empirically shows that the distribution of the instructions are more diversified after clustering.}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Proactive alignment data generation} \label{alg:pro-align} \begin{algorithmic}[1] $: a set of error-inducing instructions.} \OUTPUT{\anorm: the alignment dataset for normal programming tasks; \asec: the alignment dataset for secure coding practices.} \STATE $\anorm,\asec \leftarrow \emptyset,\emptyset$ \FOR{$l,c,i_n,i_e \in \mathcal{E}$} \STATE $y_{v} \sim \codelm(\cdot|i_e)$ \mycomment vulnerable code \STATE $y_{n} \sim \codelm(\cdot|i_n)$ \mycomment normal code \IF{$\mathcal{S}(y_v) = \emptyset$} \STATE continue \ENDIF \STATE $y_f \sim \codelm(\cdot|y_v,\mathcal{S}(y_v),i_e)$ \mycomment fixed code \IF{$\mathcal{S}(y_f) \neq \emptyset$} \STATE continue \mycomment make sure the fix is secure \ENDIF \STATE $\asec \leftarrow \asec \cup \{(i_e,y_f,y_v)\}$ \STATE $\anorm \leftarrow \anorm \cup \{(i_n,y_n,y_f)\}$ \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-103: It is known that the alignment training is more effective when the pairwise data are generated by the model to align~\cite{tang2024understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Therefore, given a code LLM to align, \toolname uses $\pi_{\theta}$ to run inference on the dataset $\mathcal{E}$ and constructs the alignment dataset.
elements.sent-2411-105: We refer the dataset construction algorithm in \toolname as a {\em proactive data generation algorithm because \toolname intentionally exposes the weakness in $\pi_{\theta}$ and applies fixes.}
elements.sent-2411-106: As shown in Algorithm~\ref{alg:pro-align , given an entry in the error-inducing instruction dataset, \toolname first uses the code LLM to implement both the error-inducing instruction and the normal instruction~(line~3--4), resulting a potentially vulnerable code snippet and a normal code snippet.}
elements.sent-2411-107: The vulnerable code are naturally the lose samples in the security alignment.
elements.sent-2411-108: However, it is not obvious how to select the win samples.
elements.sent-2411-109: For one thing, some error-inducing instructions may not has secure implementations at all.
elements.sent-2411-110: More importantly, the version with no detected errors might just be an alternative implementation for the task, but not necessarily the secure version of an implementation.
elements.sent-2411-111: We show an example in Figure~\ref{fig:why-fix .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=.8\linewidth]{figs/why-fix.pdf} \caption{An example why the code not triggering the detector does not necessarily imply secure coding practice. Suppose that the coding task is {\em Create a python program that list files under a directory}. The relevant CWE is {\em OS-Command Injection}. For the vulnerable version, if a malicious user inputs {\tt dir; rm -rf \$HOME} to the program, the program will delete all files under the home directory. A secure version should be pass the arguments as a list to the API {\tt os.system}. However, the Code LLM may write code with a constant example command, as shown in the yellow box. Although the code does not trigger OS-Command Injection, it does not guides the model how to use the {\tt os.system} API securely. } \label{fig:why-fix}

elements.sent-2411-197: In this paper, we propose \toolname in order to address the critical gap in the security alignment of code LLMs by introducing a proactive approach that effectively mitigates vulnerabilities during the post-training phase.
elements.sent-2411-198: By synthesizing vulnerability-inducing scenarios and leveraging preference learning, \toolname enhances the ability of code LLMs to generate secure code while preserving their overall utility.
elements.sent-2411-199: Our empirical results demonstrate the significant impact of \toolname in improving LLM-generated code security, offering a scalable solution applicable across diverse models, languages, and vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-200: This work provide a pathway for future research in securing AI-driven code generation, contributing to a safer and more efficient software development landscape in era of LLM.. \nocite{langley00 \bibliographystyle{icml2021 .}}

elements.sent-2411-227: While recent code-specific large language models (LLMs) have greatly enhanced their code generation capabilities, the safety of these models remains under-explored, posing potential risks as insecure code generated by these models may introduce vulnerabilities into real-world systems.
elements.sent-2411-228: Existing methods collect security-focused datasets from real-world vulnerabilities for instruction tuning in order to mitigate such issues.
elements.sent-2411-229: However, they are largely constrained by the data sparsity of vulnerable code, and have limited applicability in the multi-stage post-training workflows of modern LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-230: The scenarios synthesized by \toolname trigger 25× more vulnerable code than a normal instruction-tuning dataset, resulting in a security-focused alignment dataset 7× larger than the previous work.
elements.sent-2411-231: Experiments show that models trained with \toolname are 25.2\% to 91.4\% more secure compared to previous work without degrading models' utility.
elements.sent-2411-232: As these models are more widely deployed in productions, their safety becomes increasingly crucial.
elements.sent-2411-233: Insecure code generated by these models has been shown to introduce vulnerabilities, posing risks in real-world applications~\cite{pearce2021empirical,pearce2022asleep .}
elements.sent-2411-234: Recent studies reveal that even state-of-the-art code LLMs frequently generate insecure code~\cite{he2023large,bhatt2023purple,heinstruction , highlighting the urgent need for the alignment with secure coding practices.}
elements.sent-2411-235: Enhancing the ability of code LLMs to generate secure code necessitates additional design considerations in their post-training stages, similar to the alignment of general safety, truthfulness, and ethical considerations~\cite{ganguli2022red,liu2023training,ji2024beavertails,dubey2024llama,hurst2024gpt .}
elements.sent-2411-236: Early efforts, such as SafeCoder~\cite{heinstruction , seek to address security concerns during the instruction tuning phase by constructing datasets of vulnerable code and corresponding fixes from GitHub commits.}
elements.sent-2411-237: The security-focused dataset is then integrated with standard instruction tuning datasets to teach the pre-trained model to generate secure code while preserving utility.
elements.sent-2411-238: However, instruction tuning-based security alignment with real-world data faces two critical challenges: Sparsity of Real-World Vulnerability Data.
elements.sent-2411-239: Vulnerable code snippets in real-world programs and their fixes are often sparse and highly contextual, limiting the effectiveness and generalizability of training secure code LLMs from real-world vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-240: For instance, SafeCoder collects only 465 entries from 145 million git commits.
elements.sent-2411-241: One crucial underlying reason for the sparsity is that human programmers have already avoided most insecure practices before commits so these processes never appear in web data.
elements.sent-2411-243: Coupling security alignment with the standard instruction tuning phase restricts its utility in modern LLM training workflows.
elements.sent-2411-244: Code LLMs can undergo multi-stage post-training processes based on human/AI feedback for further performance improvements~\cite{lee2023rlaif,shao2024deepseekmath,liu2024deepseek .}
elements.sent-2411-245: Reverting to the initial instruction tuning stage for security alignment necessitates retraining, which is resource-intensive and risks discarding the benefits of prior post-training efforts.
elements.sent-2411-246: It samples from the target code LLM all normal code, vulnerable code and the corresponding fix under different generation contexts to construct preference data, and aligns the code LLM to secure coding practices with preference learning objectives, minimizing negative effects to its utility.
elements.sent-2411-247: To address the challenge imposed by the sparsity of vulnerabilities in real-world code repositories, \toolname leverages the power of prior knowledge from human and synthesized data.
elements.sent-2411-248: Specifically, \toolname synthesizes instructions that may expose the weakness of a code LLM by incorporating CWEs into a standard code instruction tuning dataset with a general LLM.
elements.sent-2411-249: Then, these instructions are further leveraged to synthesize data for security alignment training.
elements.sent-2411-250: To address the second challenge, \toolname assumes a fully post-trained target model and adopts an additional preference optimization stage for security alignment, without any intervention in previous post-training stages.
elements.sent-2411-251: Given the synthesized vulnerability-inducing and normal instruction datasets, \toolname constructs preference data for both secure coding practices and utility preservation.
elements.sent-2411-252: Moreover, \toolname incorporates heuristic and training dynamics-based data selection, leading to a unified high-quality preference dataset.
elements.sent-2411-253: Due to the generality of preference data and the independence of the extra alignment phase, \toolname can be easily integrated into various post-training pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-254: Empirically, the instructions synthesized by \toolname induce 25 times more vulnerable code than a standard instruction tuning dataset.
elements.sent-2411-255: The models trained with the dataset synthesized by \toolname are 25.2\%--91.4\% more secure than those trained with the SafeCoder dataset.
elements.sent-2411-256: We further validate that \toolname does not harm the utility of code LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-257: We develop an automatic pipeline to synthesize and select proactive security alignment data given a code LLM and vulnerability types in a programming language.
elements.sent-2411-258: The dataset is different from existing datasets that mainly include (vulnerable) code snippets, allowing easy customization to the distribution of target code LLM.
elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{figs/wf.pdf} \caption{\toolname's data synthesis and selection pipeline. (1) The instruction synthesis stage takes as input a {\em normal coding instruction} and a $\langle$language, CWE$\rangle$ pair, and produces an {\em vulnerability-inducing instruction} that may trigger the corresponding CWE. (2) The preference data collection stage samples {\em normal code} and {\em vulnerable code} snippets from the target model given the normal and vulnerability-inducing instructions respectively. The corresponding {\em fixed code} snippets are additionally sampled from target model given the vulnerable code and other feedback. The vulnerable instruction, vulnerable code, and fixed code results in $\mathcal{D}_{\text{sec}}$ in the red box. Normal instruction, normal code, and fixed code results in $\mathcal{D}_{\text{norm}}$ in the blue box. (3) The data selection stage leverages a heuristic filter and a training dynamics-based sampler to improve the quality of data in $\mathcal{D}_{\text{sec}}$ and $\mathcal{D}_{\text{norm}}$ respectively and produce the final preference dataset.} \label{fig:wf}
elements.sent-2411-259: In this section, we introduce the background and how we formulate the security alignment of code LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-260: Consider an instruction following code LLM $\pi_{\theta (y|x)=\Pi_{i}\pi_{\theta (y_i|y_{<i ,x)$ that takes user instruction $x$ and generates the code response $y$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-261: Notably, $\pi_{\theta}$ is post-trained for multiple stages with non-trivial efforts after pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-262: To ensure safe usage, the code LLM $\pi_{\theta}$ needs to effectively incorporate the understanding of certain security-related coding practices (e.g., sanitizing inputs to prevent command injections).
elements.sent-2411-263: These practices address a range of commonly encountered issues that, if neglected, can render code vulnerable to exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-264: A widely recognized framework for categorizing such issues is the Common Weakness Enumerations (CWE)~\cite{cwe , which associates each identified weakness with a set of recommended safe coding practices and common pitfalls to avoid.}
elements.sent-2411-265: We denote the set of all programming language $l$ and CWE $c$ combinations of interest as $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{cwe = \{(l^{(i) , c^{(i) )\}_{i=1 ^N$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: An empty output list implies the given code conforms with the secure coding practices of this organization.
elements.sent-2411-267: Formally, we denote the static analyzer as follows with $\mathcal{Y}$ denoting code. \begin{equation}S: \mathcal{Y} \rightarrow \emptyset \cup \mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{cwe}} \cup \mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{cwe}}^2\cup\cdots\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{cwe}}^N\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-268: The goal of security alignment in code LLMs is to reduce the likelihood of generated code being detected by the static analyzer while preserving its ability to generate functional code that follows user instructions.
elements.sent-2411-269: We consider the security alignment of code LLM as an additional offline preference optimization stage conducted after the main training process.
elements.sent-2411-270: This stage leverages a preference optimization objective under the Bradley-Terry (BT) model~\cite{bradley1952rank,rafailov2024direct : given the dataset $\mathcal{D _{p}$, the optimization process minimizes a preference loss function $\mathcal{L _{\theta : \mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{Y _w\times\mathcal{Y _l\to \mathbb{R}$, \begin{equation}\theta^* = \underset{\theta}{\arg\!\min}\sum_{(x,y_w,y_l)\in\mathcal{D}_p}{\mathcal{L}_{\theta}(x,y_w,y_l)},\end{equation} where $x$, $y_w$, and $y_l$ denote a prompt, a preferred/win response, and a less preferred/lose response.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-271: Such formulation enables seamless integration with many existing post-training pipelines and avoids retraining.
elements.sent-2411-273: At a high level, \toolname is a systematic way of synthesizing and selecting data for the preference optimization of code LLM to guarantee secure code generation while preserving utility.
elements.sent-2411-274: An overview of \toolname's data synthesis and selection pipeline is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:wf .}
elements.sent-2411-275: We discuss how \toolname synthesizes vulnerability-inducing instructions in Section~\ref{sec:method:err-inducing , how it constructs candidate preference datasets in Section~\ref{sec:method:align-data , and how to control the quality of the final alignment dataset via specialized data selection in Section~\ref{sec:method:data-selection .}}}
elements.sent-2411-276: Existing large-scale coding instruction datasets for standard programming tasks~\cite{wei2023magicoder,InfinityInstruct2024 are insufficient for this purpose, as many CWEs arise from highly specific coding scenarios underrepresented in these datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-277: For instance, CWE-79, illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:wf , refers to Cross-Site Scripting, where user inputs are embedded into web pages without proper sanitization, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary code in a victim's browser.}
elements.sent-2411-278: To reveal a code LLM's limitations in addressing CWE-79, tasks must involve writing web applications.
elements.sent-2411-279: Empirical evidence (Figure~\ref{fig:tigger-dist ) shows that only about 0.7\% of a standard instruction-tuning dataset can trigger CWEs.}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Vulnerability-inducing instruction generation} \label{alg:err-inducing} \begin{algorithmic}[1] $: a standard instruction dataset} \OUTPUT{$\mathcal{V}$: a set of vulnerability-inducing instructions. Each entry contains $l,c,x_{n},x_{v}$, denoting the programming language, the CWE, the normal instruction, and the vulnerability-inducing instruction, respectively.} \STATE $\mathcal{V} \leftarrow \emptyset$ \FOR{$l,c \in \dcwe $} \STATE $ scenario \leftarrow $ {\tt query\_cwe\_definition}$(l,c)$ \label{alg:err-inducing:scen} \STATE $\mathcal{I}_r \leftarrow $ {\tt relevant\_instruction}$(\mathcal{I}, l, c)$ \label{alg:err-inducing:rel} \STATE $\mathcal{V}_0 \leftarrow \emptyset$ \FOR{$x_n \in \mathcal{I}_r$} \STATE $x_v \leftarrow $ {\tt compose}$(x_n, scenario, l, c)$ \label{alg:err-inducing:compose} \STATE $\mathcal{V}_0 \leftarrow \mathcal{E}_0 \cup \{(l, c, x_n, x_v)\}$ \ENDFOR \STATE $\mathcal{V} \leftarrow \mathcal{V}\ \cup $ cluster$(\mathcal{V}_0, K)$ \label{alg:err-inducing:cluster} \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-280: We describe how \toolname synthesizes vulnerability-inducing instructions in Algorithm~\ref{alg:err-inducing .}
elements.sent-2411-281: Given a programming language and a CWE, \toolname queries a general knowledge-intensive LLM to enumerate program scenarios that might trigger the CWE in the corresponding language~(line~3).
elements.sent-2411-282: In addition, \toolname selects the normal instructions that are relevant to the programming language from the instruction-tuning dataset~(line~4).
elements.sent-2411-283: For each relevant normal instruction, \toolname then instructs a general LLM to compose the vulnerability-inducing instructions by combining the normal instruction with the program scenarios that may trigger the vulnerability~(line~7).
elements.sent-2411-284: We noticed the lack of diversity in LLM generated coding scenarios in our preliminary experiments.
elements.sent-2411-285: Hence, we sample multiple answers for each query with a high temperature, and cluster all instructions relevant to a language and CWE to $K$ clusters.
elements.sent-2411-286: Only the centroid of each cluster is included in the final instruction dataset, as denoted by line~10 in Algorithm~\ref{alg:err-inducing .}
elements.sent-2411-287: Figure~\ref{fig:inst-cluster empirically shows that the distribution of the instructions is more diversified after clustering.}
elements.sent-2411-288: An important observation in our experiment is that a post-trained model is able to fix an insecure code snippet, given the vulnerabilities identified in the insecure code, even though it makes mistakes with only the vulnerability-inducing instruction.
elements.sent-2411-289: Based on the observation, \toolname first collects vulnerable code snippets by sampling the target model's response on the vulnerability-inducing instructions.
elements.sent-2411-290: Then it asks the target model to fix the identified vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-291: Specifically, given a vulnerability-inducing instruction $x_v$, \toolname samples multiple responses from the target model $\pi_{\theta}$.
